item.title: JetBrains AI Key Theft, Vertex AI Bucket Squatting, Rokarolla, and REDCap Exposure
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed research infrastructure linked to espionage activity. Aikido Security and BleepingComputer reported a coordinated campaign involving at least 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants, code-review tools, and Git utilities. The plugins exfiltrated AI provider API keys entered into their settings to a hardcoded server at 39.107.60[.]51 over plain HTTP, while continuing to function as advertised. The plugins were published under seven vendor accounts and had nearly 70,000 reported installs, though Aikido noted that marketplace download counts and reviews may have been manipulated. The campaign reflects broader concern over developer machine compromise and package ecosystem abuse. StepSecurity described recent npm and PyPI attacks such as Miasma and Hades that install malicious package versions on developer workstations and harvest secrets, including registry publishing tokens. Its analysis focused on package-manager configuration files such as .npmrc, bunfig.toml, .yarnrc, .yarnrc.yml, and pip.conf as locations where effective registry settings and hardcoded credentials can expose developers and maintainers. A separate AI platform issue affected the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python, where predictable default staging bucket names enabled a bucket-squatting attack against model uploads. Unit 42 showed that an attacker with a victim project ID and region could pre-create the expected Cloud Storage bucket, receive uploaded model files, and replace them with a malicious pickle or joblib model before Vertex AI loaded it. Google addressed the issue by adding randomness to bucket names in version 1.144.0 and ownership verification in version 1.148.0. Netskope published analysis arguing that zero trust strategies are expanding beyond human users and workloads to include AI agents. The article cited Anthropic’s “Zero Trust for AI Agents” framing and emphasized agent identity as a control point for determining which agents can access which resources under specific conditions. The piece described blended identity models that combine an agent’s identity with the identity of the human invoking it, using contextual attributes such as time, location, sensitivity, risk score, and application instance. It also framed prompt injection, jailbreaking, anomalous behavior, and data exfiltration as security concerns for agent inputs and outputs. Zimperium reported a new Android banking trojan named Rokarolla targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications with 137 supported commands. The malware is distributed through malicious websites impersonating Google Chrome or TikTok downloads, then presents itself as Google Play Protect during installation. Once launched, it requests Accessibility service access along with notification, SMS, and call permissions, and uses fake overlays to steal credentials, card data, and other financial information. Kaspersky also identified malware distribution through Steam Workshop content for the Wallpaper Engine application. Malicious “application wallpapers,” which are executable Windows applications, were downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times before being reported. Observed payloads included DarkKomet, Lumma, Vidar, cryptocurrency miners, botnet loaders, RanEngine, and ransomware strains, with Steam removing the reported items after notification. Censys published an exposure analysis of REDCap following Google Threat Intelligence Group reporting on UNC6508, a PRC-nexus actor linked to a year-plus espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions. UNC6508 reportedly exploited an externally facing REDCap server, deployed a webshell, and used the INFINITERED PHP backdoor, although the initial access method remains unconfirmed. Censys observed just over 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap instances globally as of June 16, 2026, with 40% in the United States and limited adoption of the latest observed version. Health-ISAC separately reported that cyber insurance policyholders are facing heavier scrutiny in underwriting and claims despite declining rates. The article cited insurer concern over concentration in large U.S. policyholders and the potential for a major supply chain event or outage to create systemic impact. It also noted that organizations are examining continuity scenarios involving major IT outages caused by malicious activity or flawed updates.

item.title: CVE-2026-27577 n8n RCE, GhostTree Evasion, and AI Security Governance
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to address abuse, fraud, and child safety concerns, raising parallel security and privacy questions. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-27577, a critical n8n code injection vulnerability that allows an authenticated user with workflow creation or editing permissions to execute system commands on the host through crafted workflow expressions. The flaw stems from a sandbox escape in n8n’s expression and code-node evaluation environment, involving PrototypeSanitizer behavior that can expose host process internals. Affected versions include n8n before 1.123.22, versions 2.0.0 through 2.9.2, and 2.10.0 across self-hosted and n8n Cloud deployments, with fixes released on February 25, 2026. Varonis described GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless valid file path combinations. Because users with write access can create junctions without administrator privileges, GhostBranch and GhostTree structures can cause recursive scanners, including EDR products, to follow loops and fail to complete inspection of target folders. The report states that Microsoft later patched a Windows Defender bypass after initially closing the issue. Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to surface supply chain risk in project manifests, particularly missing lockfiles that leave dependency resolution unpinned. The feature was built after customer exposure analysis during the Axios npm compromise showed that risk was not limited to projects directly depending on the compromised Axios version. Socket separates these alerts from dependency alerts and reports the affected manifest, package-manager context, and impact details. Health-ISAC covered the FDA’s draft guidance for artificial intelligence-enabled medical devices, emphasizing how regulators are adapting to software that changes over time. The non-binding 2025 guidance calls out AI-specific risks including model drift, bias, and data poisoning, while expecting manufacturers to use predetermined change-control plans that preserve monitoring, testing, transparency, and auditability. Phil Englert of Health-ISAC framed the approach as recognition of AI’s evolving nature while maintaining accountability and patient safety. Censys described its integration with Google SecOps, positioning internet intelligence as enrichment for AI-enabled security operations workflows. The integration can add context about IPs, web properties, certificates, active DNS, historical observations, adversary clusters, and infrastructure relationships directly into SecOps cases and SOAR flows. The reporting emphasizes analyst pivoting from single indicators into related infrastructure and using historical lookups or rescans to understand how assets appeared during an incident window. NetSPI warned that regulated industries evaluating AI security tooling face practical constraints around determinism, auditability, trust boundaries, cost predictability, and evidentiary consistency. The article argues that many LLM-enabled security systems remain better suited as productivity enhancers for skilled practitioners than as replacements for human judgment. It also highlights budgeting uncertainty from token-based pricing and the potential mismatch between non-deterministic AI outputs and auditor or regulator expectations. The Record reported that India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of the June 21 NEET-UG medical entrance exam rerun due to concerns the platform could be used for cheating. Authorities also directed Telegram to disable message editing in India until June 30, citing scams in which edited messages made fake leaked exam questions appear authentic after the fact. The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed many Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent exam paper access, while Ahmedabad police arrested members of a cyber fraud ring accused of operating eight Telegram channels and moving about 15 million rupees through fraudulent bank accounts. BleepingComputer reported that the UK plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations expected before Christmas and implementation anticipated in spring 2027. Covered platforms are expected to include user-to-user services with algorithmic feeds such as Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook, and X, while messaging services and YouTube Kids are excluded. New accounts may require age verification through identity document uploads or facial age scans, and experts cited in the article warned that these mechanisms can be bypassed while creating risks tied to biometric and identity data collection.

item.title: Rokarolla, FortiSandbox CVEs, DragonForce Teams Relays, and LiteSpeed cPanel Exploitation
item.description: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure,...
item.content:encoded: Reporting in this window centered on active exploitation of exposed infrastructure, increasingly evasive malware tradecraft, and the continuing shift toward attack-path-based exposure management. Several items highlighted how attackers are blending legitimate services, anonymizing infrastructure, and trusted communication channels into intrusion workflows, complicating detection and attribution. Mobile banking malware, ransomware operations, phishing, cloud audit fidelity, and exploited appliance flaws all featured prominently. The coverage also reflected growing attention to continuous controls validation, operational resilience in healthcare, and AI-enabled analysis in OT environments. CISA added an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability, identified in the reporting as CVE-2026-48172, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. The flaw affects LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8 and reportedly allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure affected systems within three days under BOD 26-04 guidance. Fortinet FortiSandbox also appeared in exploitation reporting, with Defused Cyber observing activity against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089. The first two issues involve JRPC API path traversal and OS command injection, both potentially reachable through crafted HTTP requests by unauthenticated attackers. CVE-2026-25089 was patched more recently, and Defused Cyber assessed that the observed exploit attempt for that flaw appeared AI-generated and faulty, with no working public exploit known. Zimperium documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with support for 137 remote commands. The malware is distributed through malicious sites impersonating popular applications such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper posing as Google Play Protect. Once installed, it abuses Accessibility permissions to disable protections, capture lock-screen credentials, deploy fake login overlays, read and send SMS messages, log screens and keystrokes, and silently replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. DragonForce ransomware was reported abusing Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure through a custom Go-based backdoor named Backdoor.Turn. Symantec described it as the first known in-the-wild malware to use Teams relay servers to mask command-and-control traffic. In a December 2025 intrusion against a major U.S. services company, the attackers likely gained access through an unknown SQL or MSSQL server flaw, used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers to obtain kernel-level privileges, exfiltrated data, and deployed ransomware. Business email compromise reporting from IRONSCALES showed a same-day payment request sent from a legitimate construction consulting domain via hosted Exchange infrastructure. SPF passed and DMARC returned bestguesspass, while the body fabricated a prior thread using real employee names and attached a clean PDF invoice with Wells Fargo banking details. The x-originating-ip header pointed to 45[.]67[.]97[.]6 in Seoul, inconsistent with the Albuquerque-based sender, and the message was mitigated before recipient action. A Spur Intelligence survey of more than 200 practitioners found anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, present in nearly every security incident. Nearly half of surveyed organizations reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted through these services. Respondents also cited lack of IP context as a major analysis challenge, with many teams still using IP intelligence primarily after alerts are generated. Permiso highlighted risks around GCP’s deprecated serviceData field and the broader dependency of cloud detection engineering on audit log quality. The article emphasized that administrative activity in GCP is represented through audit log entries and that their structure and completeness directly affect detection rules, investigations, and post-incident reconstruction. The reporting framed log fidelity as a core constraint on reliable cloud threat detection. Tenable and XM Cyber coverage reflected the continued movement from traditional vulnerability counting toward continuous threat exposure management and attack-path prioritization. Tenable One’s continuous control validation maps defenses such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, SIEM, and DLP onto potential attack paths, while XM Cyber’s SC Awards Europe win was tied to attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and identification of choke points where a single remediation can disrupt multiple routes to critical assets. Radiflow described AI’s dual role in OT security: a force multiplier for attackers through deepfakes, automated phishing, faster scanning, and vulnerability chaining, and a defensive aid for asset visibility, threat intelligence, prioritization, and SOC efficiency. Its reporting focused on passive OT network learning through iSID and digital-twin-based breach-and-attack simulation through CIARA. In healthcare, Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego CISO Sahan Fernando emphasized operational engagement as central to cyber resilience and continuity of patient care, citing sector-wide collaboration through Health-ISAC, the Health Sector Coordinating Council, and Operation Vital Signs.

item.title: FortiSandbox, n8n, Cisco SD-WAN Exploitation and APT37 NarwhalRAT Activity
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally...
item.content:encoded: During this coverage window, reporting centered on actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, high-impact unauthenticated RCE paths, and renewed state-linked espionage tooling. A parallel theme was the growing security exposure created by automation, AI-adjacent infrastructure, and externally reachable workflow platforms, alongside regulatory pressure to produce incident context quickly. Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws were reported as under active exploitation, with Defused observing attacks against CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in the prior 24 hours. Fortinet had issued fixes for the three critical-severity vulnerabilities on April 14; the flaws allow unauthenticated attackers to escalate privileges and execute unauthorized code via low-complexity command injection without user interaction. Fortinet also previously flagged CVE-2025-61624, a medium-severity path traversal issue, as exploited in the wild. Cisco released updates for CVE-2026-20262, an actively exploited vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The flaw stems from insufficient validation during file upload and can allow an authenticated remote attacker with write access to create or overwrite files, potentially escalating privileges to root. CISA added the issue to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with a June 29, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cyera disclosed CVE-2026-21858, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability affecting locally deployed n8n instances. The issue arises from Content-Type confusion in Form webhooks, allowing attacker-controlled JSON to override trusted file metadata and copy local files such as n8n’s SQLite database or configuration into retrievable workflow data. The researchers described how those files could enable forged administrator sessions and subsequent command execution through n8n’s Execute Command node. CyCognito reported expanding external attack surface analysis to more than 60 AI stack components, including MCP servers, n8n, Ollama, MLflow, PyTorch, and Triton. The company described cases involving an exposed MCP server permitting unauthenticated natural-language access to CRM data and a public CrewAI stack where the agent API was authenticated but the RAG knowledge base remained exposed. Silent Push separately framed NIS2’s 24-hour early warning requirement as dependent on rapid infrastructure context, arguing that DNS, WHOIS, certificate, host scan, traffic, and behavioral relationship data are increasingly central to timely incident characterization. ESET reported Windows variants of SprySOCKS, previously associated with Linux malware, used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The activity was attributed with high confidence to Earth Lusca, also tracked as FishMonger and known as Aquatic Panda, Red Dev 10, and TAG-22. The Windows variants, WINDRV and WINPLUS, support TCP, UDP, and WebSocket communications, more than 30 C2 commands, SOCKS proxying, process and file management, and collection of keystrokes, clipboard contents, and active window titles. Separately, Genians reported that North Korean group ScarCruft, or APT37, used spear-phishing emails impersonating Microsoft Account security alerts to deliver NarwhalRAT. The emails referenced abnormal one-time password activity and carried ZIP archives containing malicious LNK files, which initiated a multi-stage infection chain involving batch scripts, Python retrieval, and scheduled-task persistence. NarwhalRAT supports keystroke logging, screenshots, ambient audio recording, USB data collection, directory uploads, C2 command execution, and C2 switching via Korean website relays and pCloud API functionality. Kaspersky reported malicious Wallpaper Engine packages distributed through Steam Workshop since late 2025. The campaign abused Wallpaper Engine’s application wallpaper feature, which allows Windows-compatible programs to run as desktop backgrounds, and some malicious wallpapers had thousands or tens of thousands of downloads. One analyzed sample dropped the DarkKomet backdoor as Synaptics.exe, used a modified AggregatorHost.dll to locate and hijack live Steam sessions, and exfiltrated data to 120.48.156[.]17. iRhythm Holdings disclosed a data breach involving patient personal and health information stored in third-party-hosted business applications. The company said a threat actor contacted it on June 9, 2026, claimed possession of sensitive data, and demanded payment to prevent disclosure. iRhythm attributed access to social engineering and said it had no evidence that products, clinical or medical device systems, patient safety, manufacturing and distribution, or financial reporting systems were affected.

item.title: Mitiga Skillgate Finds AI Agent Instruction File Abuse and 1,230 Leaking Keys
item.description: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt...
item.content:encoded: This digest covers a focused but significant development in the emerging security landscape around AI agents and developer-assistant ecosystems. Mitiga Labs reported that AI instruction files and agent configuration artifacts are increasingly showing malware-like tradecraft, including prompt exfiltration, model endpoint hijacking, permission bypasses, and exposed credentials. Mitiga Labs published research based on a six-month investigation into AI agent artifacts such as Cursor rules, Anthropic Skills, Claude Hooks, AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, MCP server configurations, package.json droppers, and hidden .pyc bytecode files. The findings indicate that files intended to guide or configure AI agents can also carry techniques associated with spyware, credential exposure, execution abuse, and supply-chain manipulation. The research identified prompt-exfiltration tradecraft, attacker-controlled ANTHROPICBASEURL overrides, and permission-bypass defaults embedded in AI instruction and configuration files. The ANTHROPICBASEURL behavior is particularly notable because it can redirect model traffic toward attacker-controlled infrastructure when agent environments honor the variable, creating an opportunity for interception or manipulation of AI workflows. Mitiga also reported finding more than 1,230 hardcoded API keys and JWT tokens across the examined public repository content. The company said it manually verified in-the-wild findings and did not access or authenticate with the discovered credentials, framing the exposure as part of a broader pattern of sensitive data and control logic being embedded in artifacts consumed by AI coding assistants and agentic systems. Alongside the research, Mitiga Labs released Skillgate, a free scanner designed to detect risky patterns across AI instruction files, skills, hooks, agent brain files, MCP configurations, supply-chain droppers, and bytecode artifacts. According to Mitiga, Skillgate has scanned more than 50,000 files across over 7,000 public repositories. The tool currently includes more than 80 detection rules organized into technique families including direct execution, prompt manipulation, MCP server poisoning, supply-chain attacks, obfuscation, and credential exposure. This rule structure reflects how AI agent ecosystems are beginning to develop their own recognizable abuse patterns, overlapping with traditional malware and software supply-chain techniques while adding AI-specific vectors such as prompt manipulation and agent instruction poisoning. The reporting highlights a shift in attacker opportunity from conventional source code alone to the broader operational context surrounding AI-assisted development. As AI agents increasingly ingest repository-level instructions and configuration files, those artifacts become part of the executable or decision-influencing environment, expanding the set of files that may affect developer workflows, automation behavior, and sensitive credential handling.

item.title: JetBrains AI Key Theft, Rokarolla, FortiSandbox Exploits, and DragonForce Teams C2
item.description: This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply chain abuse, cloud and edge-product exploitation, and malware campaigns that blend into trusted platforms. Reporting also highlights how AI adoption is reshaping both attacker tradecraft and enterprise security governance, from stolen LLM API keys...
item.content:encoded:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply chain abuse, cloud and edge-product exploitation, and malware campaigns that blend into trusted platforms. Reporting also highlights how AI adoption is reshaping both attacker tradecraft and enterprise security governance, from stolen LLM API keys to agent identity and auditability concerns. Aikido Security and BleepingComputer reported a coordinated JetBrains Marketplace campaign involving at least 15 malicious IDE plugins installed close to 70,000 times. The plugins posed as AI coding assistants, code-review tools, and Git utilities for services such as OpenAI, DeepSeek, and SiliconFlow, exfiltrating API keys over plain HTTP to 39.107.60[.]51 when users saved plugin settings. Several supply chain articles focused on exposure at developer endpoints rather than only CI/CD systems. StepSecurity described package-manager configuration drift across npm, pnpm, bun, yarn, and pip, while Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to flag missing lockfiles after incidents such as the Axios compromise showed that unpinned dependency trees can expand blast radius beyond direct dependencies. AI governance and AI security tooling remained a parallel theme. Netskope discussed applying zero trust concepts to AI agents with blended human-agent identity, while NetSPI argued that regulated industries need AI security tools to provide auditability, determinism, and predictable operational cost rather than just offensive capability demonstrations. Unit 42 detailed a Google Vertex AI SDK for Python flaw that enabled bucket squatting against predictable temporary Cloud Storage bucket names. An attacker could pre-create the expected bucket, receive model uploads, replace files with malicious pickle or joblib content, and execute code in Google-managed serving infrastructure; Google addressed the issue in SDK versions 1.144.0 and 1.148.0. Multiple reports covered active or emerging exploitation of internet-facing products. CyCognito described CVE-2026-27577, a critical authenticated n8n workflow expression sandbox escape affecting self-hosted and cloud deployments, while CISA added CVE-2026-48172 in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. The Hacker News also reported exploitation of Fortinet FortiSandbox flaws CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089, with one exploit attempt described as faulty and potentially AI-generated. Censys analyzed more than 8,500 internet-facing REDCap instances following Google Threat Intelligence Group attribution of a year-plus UNC6508 espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions. Separately, Permiso highlighted risks around deprecated GCP serviceData fields and the dependence of cloud detection engineering on audit-log fidelity. Rokarolla emerged as a new Android banking trojan targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps with 137 supported commands. Zimperium reported that it spreads through malicious sites impersonating Chrome or TikTok, abuses Accessibility services, deploys fake overlays, captures PINs and SMS codes, and can replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses. ClickFix activity continued to diversify, with campaigns delivering BabaDeda Loader, Lorem Ipsum Loader, and Potemkin through fake update and user-instruction lures. The technique remains centered on persuading users to execute attacker-provided commands through familiar Windows workflows, leading to stealers, remote access trojans, EtherRAT, and RMMProject. Trusted platforms were also used for malware staging and concealment. Kaspersky found Steam Workshop wallpaper packages for Wallpaper Engine delivering backdoors, infostealers, miners, botnet loaders, and ransomware, while Symantec reported DragonForce ransomware using Backdoor.Turn to tunnel command-and-control through Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure. Varonis described GhostTree, a Windows NTFS junction technique that can create recursive path loops and interfere with recursive scanning by security tools. The FTC reported record 2025 U.S. losses of $3.5 billion to imposter scams, with social media linked to more than $2.1 billion in losses. A separate Ironscales case study showed business email invoice fraud using a legitimate domain, passing SPF, a fabricated reply chain, and a clean PDF invoice, with the main anomaly appearing in an originating IP geolocated to South Korea. Spur Intelligence survey data indicated that anonymized infrastructure such as VPNs and residential proxies appears in the vast majority of incidents, especially account takeover and credential abuse cases. Respondents cited lack of IP context as a major operational challenge and reported that many teams still apply IP intelligence mainly after alerts are generated. India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of a medical entrance exam rerun, citing cheating and fraud concerns involving channels, groups, and bots advertising leaked exam papers. In the UK, planned under-16 social media restrictions are expected to require age verification methods such as ID uploads or facial scans, raising security and privacy concerns around identity and biometric data collection. Healthcare and regulated-sector reporting emphasized resilience, accountability, and operational continuity. Health-ISAC articles covered cyber insurance scrutiny, FDA draft guidance for AI-enabled medical devices, and healthcare resilience efforts centered on maintaining patient care during cyber disruption. Exposure-management vendors continued to frame vulnerability management around attack paths and control validation. Tenable described continuous control validation in CTEM, XM Cyber highlighted attack-path-led prioritization and choke points, and Censys detailed Google SecOps enrichment workflows that connect indicators to internet-facing infrastructure, certificates, DNS, and historical observations.

CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks — Must Read

CISA ordered U.S. federal agencies to patch an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel plugin flaw within three days, warning it can let attackers with FTP or web shell access gain root privileges on affected shared hosting servers.

  • CISA ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to secure systems within three days against an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin vulnerability.
  • The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-48172, allows attackers with FTP or web shell access to escalate privileges to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux/CageFS.
  • LiteSpeed said the flaw affects all user-end plugin versions before 2.4.8 and stems from a UNIX symlink following weakness.
  • LiteSpeed released urgent security updates in early June and advised users to update the cPanel user-end plugin to the latest version.
  • CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog and said agencies must follow BOD 26-04 guidance or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable.

Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting three critical Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities, including one patched only last week, that can enable authentication bypass and unauthenticated command execution. Organizations using affected FortiSandbox products should apply Fortinet’s patches immediately.

  • Defused Cyber said it observed attackers exploiting CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089 in Fortinet FortiSandbox over the past 24 hours.
  • CVE-2026-39813 is a FortiSandbox JRPC API path traversal vulnerability that could allow an unauthenticated attacker to bypass authentication using specially crafted HTTP requests.
  • CVE-2026-39808 is an operating system command injection flaw that could allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute unauthorized code or commands through crafted HTTP requests.
  • Fortinet patched CVE-2026-39813 and CVE-2026-39808 in April 2026, while CVE-2026-25089 was fixed last week.
  • Defused Cyber said the exploit for CVE-2026-25089 appears to have been developed using an AI model and is faulty, and no working exploit for that vulnerability has been publicly disclosed.

Ransomware gang abuses Microsoft Teams relays to hide malicious traffic — Must Read

DragonForce ransomware deployed a Go-based backdoor called Backdoor.Turn that hides command-and-control traffic by abusing Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure. Symantec says this is the first known real-world malware use of the technique, seen in a sophisticated attack on a major U.S. services company.

  • DragonForce ransomware used a custom Go-based malware named Backdoor.Turn to hide command-and-control traffic inside Microsoft Teams relay infrastructure.
  • Symantec says Backdoor.Turn is the first known in-the-wild malware to abuse Microsoft Teams TURN relay servers for command-and-control communications.
  • The December 2025 attack against a major U.S. services company likely began with exploitation of an unknown flaw in an SQL or MSSQL server.
  • The attackers used BYOVD techniques with multiple vulnerable drivers, including Huawei’s HWAuidoOs2Ec.sys, Topaz Antifraud wsftprm.sys, Tower of Fantasy GameDriverx64.sys, and K7 Security K7RKScan.sys, to gain kernel-level privileges and terminate security tools.
  • Backdoor.Turn can execute commands, create processes, scan networks, capture TLS certificates, search LDAP/Active Directory, collect website titles, and steal browser credentials.

REDCap on the Internet: An Exposure Analysis — Should Read

Censys found more than 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap servers worldwide after Google linked a year-long PRC-nexus espionage campaign to exploitation of a public REDCap instance. Many deployments appear to be running older versions, making rapid inventory, patching, MFA, and proper server isolation critical for research and medical organizations.

  • Google’s Threat Intelligence Group attributed a year-plus espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions to UNC6508, a PRC-nexus actor.
  • UNC6508 used an externally facing REDCap server as the consistent initial access vector and deployed a webshell and INFINITERED, a PHP backdoor, although the initial access method is unconfirmed.
  • As of June 16, 2026, Censys observed just over 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap instances globally, with the largest concentrations in the United States, the United Kingdom, Germany, and Australia.
  • REDCap version 16.0.17 represented just over 30% of observed Internet-facing deployments, while version 17.1.3 appeared to be the latest available version and was observed on only 1.18% of instances.
  • The article recommends that REDCap operators inventory their instances, patch to the latest available version, follow infrastructure security best practices, and enforce multi-factor authentication on administrator accounts.

ClickFix Campaigns Expand Malware Delivery With New Loaders and Fake Update Lures — Should Read

ClickFix social-engineering campaigns are expanding to deliver multiple new or evolving loaders, including BabaDeda Loader, Lorem Ipsum Loader, and Potemkin, which then deploy stealers, RATs, and ransomware-linked tooling. The key risk is that attackers are rapidly adapting delivery methods around simple user prompts to run malicious commands, making ClickFix an effective initial access technique across Windows and macOS.

  • Cybersecurity researchers reported multiple ClickFix campaigns delivering three malware loaders named BabaDeda Loader, Lorem Ipsum Loader, and Potemkin.
  • BabaDeda Loader campaigns observed in April 2026 targeted education and financial organizations and used attacker-supplied PowerShell commands to deliver stealers and remote access trojans.
  • Lorem Ipsum Loader was delivered through compromised WordPress sites using ClickFix-style Microsoft Edge security update lures, and BlueVoyant attributed the ecosystem with high confidence to Vanilla Tempest.
  • Huntress reported a ClickFix attack chain that installed an MSI package and dropped the Potemkin loader, which served as a conduit for EtherRAT and RMMProject.
  • The article states that ClickFix remains effective because it persuades users to follow authoritative-looking instructions to run commands such as pressing Win+R, pasting a command, and hitting Enter.

Emerging Threat: (CVE-2026-27577) n8n Remote Code Execution via Workflow Expressions — Should Read

CVE-2026-27577 is a critical n8n sandbox-escape vulnerability that lets authenticated workflow editors execute commands on the host, putting exposed self-hosted and cloud instances at risk. Organizations should upgrade to patched versions 1.123.22, 2.9.3, or 2.10.1 or later and restrict access to n8n instances.

  • CVE-2026-27577 is a critical code injection flaw in n8n that allows an authenticated user with workflow create or edit permissions to run system commands on the host through crafted workflow expressions.
  • The vulnerability is caused by a sandbox escape in n8n’s expression and code-node evaluation environment, involving a flaw in the PrototypeSanitizer that can expose host process internals.
  • Affected versions include n8n versions before 1.123.22, versions 2.0.0 through 2.9.2, and version 2.10.0, covering both self-hosted deployments and n8n Cloud instances.
  • n8n released patches on February 25, 2026, and affected users should upgrade to version 1.123.22, 2.9.3, 2.10.1, or later.
  • The article recommends inventorying all n8n instances, restricting workflow creation and editing permissions, removing direct internet exposure, using least-privilege service accounts, limiting outbound connectivity, and monitoring execution logs.

Steam Workshop abused to spread malware via Wallpaper Engine app — Should Read

Threat actors are abusing Steam Workshop and Wallpaper Engine’s executable “application wallpapers” to deliver malware that steals Steam credentials, installs backdoors, runs miners, and deploys infostealers. Steam removed the samples Kaspersky found, but users should only install trusted Workshop content and scan downloads with updated antivirus tools.

  • Threat actors are abusing Steam Workshop to distribute malware hidden in wallpaper packages for the Wallpaper Engine application on Steam.
  • Kaspersky researchers found dozens of malicious application wallpapers on Steam Workshop, with some downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times.
  • The malicious wallpapers can execute payloads automatically when installed and have been observed delivering backdoors, infostealers, cryptocurrency miners, botnet loaders, RanEngine, and ransomware strains.
  • In one tested case, a wallpaper posing as a game called NTRaholic installed a DarkKomet backdoor and a custom AggregatorHost.dll library designed to search for Steam accounts and steal credentials.
  • Steam identified and removed all malicious wallpaper applications reported by Kaspersky, but Kaspersky warned that threat actors are likely to submit new ones.

Multiple JetBrains IDE plugins caught stealing AI keys — Should Read

A coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace used at least 15 fake AI assistant plugins to steal users’ AI provider API keys when they were entered into plugin settings. The plugins, installed nearly 70,000 times, exfiltrated keys to a hardcoded attacker server and may have resold stolen access to paying users.

  • Aikido detected a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 IDE plugins published under seven vendor accounts.
  • The affected plugins pose as AI coding assistants for DeepSeek and other large language models and have been installed close to 70,000 times.
  • When a user enters an AI provider API key into a plugin’s settings and clicks Apply, the plugin stores the key and forwards it to a hardcoded server at 39.107.60[.]51 over plain HTTP.
  • The article lists OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek as examples of AI providers whose API keys users may enter into the affected plugins.
  • The affected plugins also include a paid tier in which the server sends an API key back to the client, and the plugin uses that server-supplied key for model calls.

Google Vertex AI SDK Flaw Let Attackers Hijack Model Uploads via Bucket Squatting — Should Read

A flaw in the Google Cloud Vertex AI Python SDK allowed attackers to squat predictable staging bucket names, hijack model uploads, and swap in malicious pickle/joblib models that executed code in Google’s serving infrastructure. Google has patched the issue, and users should update to google-cloud-aiplatform 1.148.0 or later and set a staging bucket they control.

  • A flaw in the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python allowed an attacker with no access to a victim’s project to hijack machine learning model uploads and run code inside Google’s serving infrastructure.
  • The vulnerability occurred because the SDK generated predictable temporary Cloud Storage bucket names from the project ID and region and checked whether the bucket existed but not whether the victim owned it.
  • Unit 42 said an attacker could create the expected bucket first, receive the victim’s uploaded model files, and replace them with a malicious pickle or joblib model before Vertex AI loaded the file.
  • In Unit 42’s proof of concept, the malicious payload stole an OAuth token from the serving container’s metadata server and used it to access other resources in the same Google-managed tenant project.
  • Google fixed the issue by adding randomness to bucket names in version 1.144.0 and bucket ownership verification in version 1.148.0, and users are advised to update to 1.148.0 or later.

GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware — Should Read

Varonis described “GhostTree,” a technique that uses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless Windows file paths that can cause recursive scans, including some endpoint security scans, to hang and miss malware in the folder. Microsoft has since patched the reported Windows Defender bypass issue.

  • Varonis described GhostTree, a technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless valid Windows file paths.
  • The technique works because any user with write access can create NTFS junctions without administrator privileges.
  • GhostBranch uses one child junction that points back to its parent directory, while GhostTree uses multiple child junctions to create many more possible path combinations.
  • Varonis said recursive scanning tools, including EDR products, can follow these junction loops and fail to finish scanning the folder, leaving malicious files unexamined.
  • Varonis reported the issue to Microsoft, and the article states that Microsoft later patched the Windows Defender bypass despite initially closing the ticket.

New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds — Should Read

Zimperium researchers found Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan spread through fake app sites that abuses Accessibility access to steal PINs, SMS codes, banking credentials, and crypto funds. Its 137 remote commands let attackers disable Play Protect, overlay fake login screens, intercept messages, rewrite crypto wallet addresses, and remotely control infected phones.

  • Zimperium's zLabs documented Rokarolla, a new Android banking trojan that targets 217 banking and cryptocurrency apps and supports 137 remote commands.
  • Rokarolla spreads through malicious websites posing as well-known apps such as TikTok and Chrome, with an initial dropper pretending to be Google Play Protect.
  • The malware abuses Accessibility access to disable Google Play Protect, capture lock-screen PINs or passwords, deploy fake HTML login overlays, and take screenshots without using MediaProjection.
  • Rokarolla can read and send SMS messages, make itself the default app for texts and calls, block incoming calls, scrape contacts, read notifications, and log keystrokes and screens.
  • The trojan silently rewrites clipboard contents to replace copied cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses.

FTC warns of record $3.5 billion losses to imposter scams in 2025 — Should Read

The FTC says Americans lost a record $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, with social media driving a rapidly growing share of losses. Business and government impersonation scams, especially fake bank alerts, remain among the costliest schemes.

  • The FTC warned that Americans lost $3.5 billion to imposter scams in 2025, with reported losses nearly tripling since 2020.
  • Imposter scams were the most reported fraud category in 2025, accounting for nearly one in three fraud reports filed with the FTC.
  • Victims lost nearly $1 billion to business impersonators and approximately $920 million to government impersonators, with fake bank security alerts among the costliest schemes.
  • Social media was linked to more than $2.1 billion in 2025 losses from imposter scams, and nearly one in three victims who lost money were first contacted through social media.
  • Since its Impersonation Rule took effect in April 2024, the FTC has brought a dozen enforcement actions, secured more than $70 million in consumer redress, and halted some imposter schemes.

Prevent npm and Python Supply Chain Attacks on Developer Machines with Package Configs — Should Read

StepSecurity launched Package Configs for Dev Machine Guard to show whether developer machines are actually using protected npm and PyPI registries, cooldown policies, and safe credential handling. The key takeaway is that supply chain defenses only work if every endpoint is correctly configured, because one laptop pointed at the public registry with exposed tokens can let worms like Miasma or Hades spread.

  • Recent npm and PyPI supply chain attacks, including the axios and TanStack compromises, Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades, involved trusted packages shipping malicious versions that installed on developer machines and stole secrets.
  • StepSecurity says Dev Machine Guard's new Package Configs feature inventories package-manager configuration across developer machines to show which registries packages resolve from, whether cooldown is in effect, and where registry credentials are exposed.
  • Package Configs audits .npmrc, bunfig.toml, .yarnrc, .yarnrc.yml, and pip.conf files for npm, pnpm, bun, yarn, and pip across global, user, and project scopes.
  • The article states that cooldown policies and internal or Secure Registries can help block newly published or known-compromised package versions, but only when developer machines are configured to use them.
  • Package Configs flags machines that resolve directly from public registries, lack cooldown support or configuration, or contain hardcoded registry tokens in configuration files.

UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts — Should Read

The UK plans to ban under-16s from major social media platforms by spring 2027, effectively requiring new users to prove their age with ID uploads or facial scans. Security and privacy experts warn the checks are easy to bypass with VPNs while creating major risks for identity and biometric data.

  • The UK government plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations due before Christmas and the rules expected to take effect in spring 2027.
  • Platforms covered by the ban will include user-to-user services with algorithmic feeds such as Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and X, while messaging services such as WhatsApp and Signal and YouTube Kids are excluded.
  • New social media accounts in the UK will likely require age verification through methods such as ID uploads or facial age scans, while many long-standing accounts may be treated as low-risk and avoid fresh checks.
  • The article says VPN use can sidestep age checks because the Online Safety Act targets sites rather than users, and Australia’s under-16 ban has reportedly still left more than 60% of children using social media months after implementation.
  • Security and privacy experts cited in the article warn that age verification methods can be bypassed by motivated minors and that collecting identity documents or biometric data creates risks of identity theft, blackmail and data breaches.

India temporarily blocks Telegram over medical exam cheating fears — May Read

India temporarily blocked Telegram and disabled its message-editing feature ahead of a nationwide NEET-UG medical exam rerun, citing fears the platform could be used to spread fake or fraudulent leaked-paper claims. Digital rights advocates criticized the move as overbroad and unlikely to address the underlying causes of exam fraud.

  • India temporarily blocked access to Telegram nationwide ahead of the June 21 rerun of the NEET-UG medical entrance exam over fears it could be used for cheating.
  • Indian authorities also directed Telegram to disable its message-editing feature in India until June 30, saying scammers had used it to make fake leaked exam questions appear authentic after the fact.
  • The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed a substantial number of Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent access to supposedly leaked exam papers.
  • Police in Ahmedabad arrested members of a cyber fraud ring accused of operating eight Telegram channels and moving about 15 million rupees through fraudulent bank accounts.
  • The Internet Freedom Foundation criticized the Telegram restrictions as reactive and ineffective, arguing they punished ordinary users and did not address vulnerabilities in the examination system.

GCP's serviceData: Officially Deprecated, Actively Dangerous — May Read

GCP’s deprecated serviceData audit log field is still present and can be misleading or unsafe for detection engineering. Security teams should avoid relying on it and validate their GCP detections against supported audit log fields.

  • The article states that GCP's serviceData is officially deprecated and actively dangerous.
  • Cloud audit logs are described as the backbone of detection engineering in cloud environments.
  • Security teams rely on cloud audit logs to identify suspicious behavior, build detection rules, and reconstruct attacker activity after an incident.
  • The article states that in GCP, every meaningful administrative action leaves a trace as an audit log entry.
  • The quality of GCP audit log entries directly affects the reliability of investigative and detection efforts.

The Invoice That Originated from the Wrong Continent — May Read

A convincing invoice fraud email from a legitimate construction consulting domain passed SPF and appeared authentic, but header inspection revealed it was submitted from an anomalous South Korean IP address inconsistent with the sender’s US-based company. The case shows how BEC payment diversion can evade standard email authentication and rely on subtle behavioral or header anomalies for detection.

  • A same-day payment request arrived from the legitimate construction consulting domain adacen.com via hosted Exchange infrastructure, with SPF passing and DMARC returning bestguesspass.
  • The email body fabricated a three-message reply chain using real names and directed the recipient to complete payment by close of business.
  • The attached PDF invoice included line items, a payment amount, and Wells Fargo routing and account numbers, while static analysis found the PDF clean.
  • The key anomaly was the x-originating-ip header showing 45.67.97.6, geolocated to Seoul, South Korea, with no PTR record, despite the sender domain belonging to an Albuquerque-based firm.
  • Themis flagged the message at 62% confidence with a VIP Recipient label and triggered inline mitigation before the recipient could act.

How FDA’s Draft Guidance Shapes AI Medical Device Safety — Optional

FDA’s 2025 draft guidance pushes makers of AI-enabled medical devices to manage evolving risks such as model drift, bias and data poisoning through predefined change-control plans, monitoring, testing and auditability. The key takeaway is that regulators are allowing AI models to change over time, but only with stronger safeguards for patient safety and accountability.

  • The FDA’s draft guidance for artificial intelligence-enabled medical devices changes how regulators address software that evolves over time.
  • Phil Englert of Health-ISAC said the framework recognizes AI’s ability to evolve while emphasizing patient safety, transparency and accountability.
  • The FDA’s 2025 non-binding guidance says manufacturers must account for AI-specific risks including model drift, bias and data poisoning.
  • The FDA expects organizations to use predetermined change-control plans that support monitoring, testing and auditability rather than requiring static models.

Introducing Manifest Alerts — Optional

Socket launched Manifest Alerts to flag repositories that have package manifests but no lockfiles, making dependency installs non-reproducible and harder to verify after supply chain incidents. The feature provides remediation guidance for generating lockfiles with npm, pnpm, Yarn, or Bun.

  • Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to detect missing lockfiles and surface supply chain risks found in project manifests.
  • The feature was built in response to customer challenges during the Axios npm compromise, where exposure was not limited to projects that directly depended on the compromised Axios version.
  • A missing lockfile means dependency versions are not pinned, so fresh installs can resolve different dependency trees depending on registry state and timing.
  • Socket separates Manifest Alerts from Dependency Alerts, with Manifest Alerts identifying the affected manifest and explaining that the dependency tree is not pinned.
  • The alert drawer provides impact details, package-manager-specific remediation guidance for npm, pnpm, yarn, or bun, and options to ignore the alert or create a Jira or Linear ticket.

Mythic Embarking on the Open Seas: Containerized Payload Delivery for Kubernetes Assessments — Optional

SpecterOps released two Mythic extensions, container_wrapper and container_registry, that let operators package Mythic payloads into OCI-compatible containers and push them to a self-hosted registry for Kubernetes assessments. The goal is to streamline ceded-access workflows where clients deploy a containerized payload in their cluster via a simple Kubernetes manifest.

  • SpecterOps created two Mythic extensions, container_wrapper and container_registry, to simplify container-centric Kubernetes and Docker-based ceded access workflows.
  • The container_wrapper payload type lets operators select an existing Poseidon payload in the Mythic web UI, add it to a chosen base container image, and produce an OCI-compatible tar file.
  • The container_registry third-party service agent lets operators upload the OCI-compatible tar file to a self-hosted container registry from Mythic.
  • The article recommends placing a distribution-based registry behind an HTTPS reverse proxy with TLS and read-only access controls for pull workflows.
  • Operators can provide clients with a Kubernetes manifest that deploys the hosted container image in a Kubernetes cluster, assuming worker nodes have internet egress and no policy restrictions block the registry.

Rady Children’s Fernando Says Operational Engagement Is the Key to Cyber Resilience — Optional

Rady Children’s CISO Sahan Fernando argues that healthcare cyber resilience depends on security teams deeply understanding hospital operations, not just deploying technology. He highlights broad sector collaboration through Health-ISAC, HSCC, and Operation Vital Signs to keep patient care running during disruptions.

  • Sahan Fernando, Chief Information Security Officer at Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego, says healthcare cyber resilience depends on whether security teams understand how hospitals operate each day.
  • Fernando argues that deep operational engagement is the foundation of programs designed to keep patient care running through disruption.
  • Fernando is participating in Operation Vital Signs and said he is excited to see how it is carried out with many participants across the United States.
  • Fernando serves on the Board of Health-ISAC and said there is significant interest across the health sector.
  • Fernando said Health-ISAC and the Health Sector Coordinating Council bring together providers, pharmaceuticals, lab sciences, payers, and others involved in patient care.

Survey: 94% of Incidents Involve Anonymized Infrastructure. Teams Are Still Reactive — Optional

A Spur Intelligence survey found that anonymized infrastructure like VPNs and residential proxies now appears in nearly every security incident, but many security teams still lack the context and workflows to act on IP data proactively. The key takeaway is that organizations need richer IP intelligence integrated into real-time risk decisions, not just post-incident investigations.

  • A Spur Intelligence study of more than 200 security practitioners found that anonymizing infrastructure, including VPNs and residential proxy networks, appears in nearly every security incident.
  • Nearly half of surveyed companies reported significant operational or financial impact from account takeover attempts and credential abuse conducted via VPNs and residential proxies.
  • Nearly half of respondents said lack of context is the biggest challenge for security teams analyzing IP activity.
  • Many organizations primarily use IP intelligence during investigations after alerts have already been generated, but respondents want more predictive and intelligence-led workflows.
  • A full third of companies surveyed are not measuring the effectiveness of their IP intelligence efforts at all.

Beyond the Hype: What Regulated Industries Need to Know Before Trusting AI Security Tooling — Optional

AI security tools can help skilled security teams work faster, but regulated industries should not trust fully autonomous tools without strong guardrails, auditability, cost controls, and human validation. The biggest risks are inconsistent results, unclear ROI, rising model costs, and outputs that may not satisfy auditors or regulators.

  • NetSPI says enterprise security teams in regulated industries need AI security tooling to provide consistency, reliability, auditability, and predictable return on investment rather than simply demonstrating attack-building capability.
  • The article argues that most LLM-enabled security systems still lack the infrastructure and controls needed for determinism, auditability, error handling, and well-defined trust boundaries in enterprise environments.
  • NetSPI states that AI security tools can function as productivity enhancers for skilled practitioners when used with operational guardrails, but should not replace human judgment or creativity.
  • The article says AI security tooling can create budgeting problems because token-based costs and vendor pricing changes may make operational deployments much more expensive than initial projections.
  • The article argues that AI-only security testing often may not meet auditor and regulator expectations because non-deterministic outputs can produce inconsistent evidence.

Agents Deserve A Place In Every Zero Trust Strategy — Optional

The article argues that zero trust strategies must extend to AI agents by giving them verifiable identities, least-privilege access, continuous monitoring, and runtime guardrails. Netskope positions its platform and partners as a way to provide visibility, policy enforcement, and controls for agentic AI usage.

  • The article argues that zero trust principles should be extended to AI agents so that the right people and agents have the right access to the right resources at the right times for the right reasons.
  • Anthropic’s “Zero Trust for AI Agents” eBook recommends defenses that make attacks impossible rather than merely tedious, because agentic adversaries can persist continuously without getting bored.
  • The article says agents need identities, and Netskope’s partnership with Aembit can identify and authenticate agents using short-lived credentials that blend agent identity with the human invoker.
  • Netskope policies can use blended identity and context such as time, location, sensitivity, risk score, and application instance to enforce least-agency access controls for agents.
  • The article states that Netskope can observe AI traffic to discover AI applications and MCP servers, detect shadow AI usage, maintain AI asset inventories, and monitor agent behavior for anomalies.

Powering the AI-Enabled SOC with Censys Internet Intelligence and Google SecOps — Optional

Censys is promoting its Google SecOps integration, which enriches SOC alerts and investigations with real-time and historical external internet intelligence about IPs, domains, certificates, and related infrastructure. The core takeaway is that high-quality infrastructure context can make AI-assisted triage, threat hunting, detection engineering, incident response, and rescans faster and more accurate.

  • Censys provides Google SecOps users with real-time context about IPs, web properties, certificates, active DNS, historical data, adversary clusters, and infrastructure relationships.
  • The Censys for Google SecOps SOAR integration can automatically enrich alerts and cases with external internet intelligence directly inside SecOps workflows.
  • Censys enrichment can support faster alert triage by returning host details, exposed services, certificates, web technologies, security configurations, and reputation scores for indicators.
  • The integration supports threat hunting and detection engineering by helping analysts pivot from a single indicator to related infrastructure and identify observable infrastructure patterns.
  • Censys historical lookups and rescans help incident responders investigate how infrastructure appeared during an incident window and refresh observations when current data is needed.

The VM Category Shift: XM Cyber Wins SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution — Optional

XM Cyber won SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution, signaling a shift from traditional scan-and-patch vulnerability management toward attack-path-led exposure management. The key takeaway is that teams should prioritize vulnerabilities based on whether attackers can use them to reach critical assets, not just severity scores.

  • XM Cyber won the SC Awards Europe 2026 Best Vulnerability Management Solution after Qualys had won the category for three consecutive years.
  • The SC Awards judges praised XM Cyber for differentiation from conventional vulnerability management tools through attack simulation, attack graph analysis, and CTEM-aligned methodologies.
  • The article says attack-path-led vulnerability management prioritizes vulnerabilities that sit on viable routes from initial access to critical assets and sets aside vulnerabilities that are dead ends.
  • The judges highlighted XM Cyber’s ability to identify choke points, where a single fix can disrupt multiple attack paths at once.
  • XM Cyber plans to add Discovery Scanning, Network Vulnerability Scanning, Cryptography Scanning, and Container and Cloud Workloads Vulnerability Scanning to feed into its PostureDNA.

Cyber insurance policyholders facing heavier scrutiny in underwriting, claims — Optional

Cyber insurance policyholders are facing tougher underwriting and claims scrutiny as falling rates and fears of large systemic outages push insurers to add more restrictions and exclusions. Organizations are focusing more on resilience planning to keep critical systems running during major IT failures or cyber events.

  • Enterprises with cyber insurance policies are facing greater scrutiny in underwriting and claims as rates decline and insurers try to remain profitable.
  • Cyber insurers are concerned that their business is overly dependent on large U.S. policyholders, which account for nearly two-thirds of the global market share.
  • Insurers fear that a major supply chain event or outage could escalate and potentially threaten the cyber insurance industry as a whole.
  • A multiyear lull in insurance rates and insurers’ dependence on large U.S. policyholders have contributed to more restrictions and exclusions in coverage.
  • Errol Weiss of Health-ISAC said organizations are evaluating how to keep major systems running if major IT systems go down due to a malicious actor or a bad update.

Improving precision in CTEM: How continuous controls validation in Tenable One transforms exposure management — Optional

Tenable says continuous control validation in Tenable One helps CTEM teams focus on attack paths that are actually reachable and exploitable by factoring in defenses like EDR, MFA and firewalls. The main takeaway is to prioritize validated exposure over theoretical vulnerability lists, especially as AI accelerates vulnerability discovery.

  • The article says organizations need to shift from managing theoretical cyber risks to validating actual, accessible exposure because vulnerability exploitation is a top initial access vector and frontier AI is accelerating vulnerability discovery.
  • Tenable One adds continuous control validation by mapping active controls such as EDR, MFA, firewalls, and DLP onto potential attack paths to help deprioritize weaknesses that existing defenses neutralize.
  • Tenable One allows analysts to filter top attack paths based on the presence of security controls and whether attack chains can be prevented, supporting faster triage and investigation.
  • The Tenable One Open Connector can ingest penetration testing results and layer them with real-time exposure insights to identify toxic risk combinations affecting critical business assets.
  • The article states that exposure management context should combine security control validation with asset criticality, threat activity, entitlement privileges, and attack pathways to prioritize the most critical risks.

item.title: UK Plans Under-16 Social Media Ban and Age Assurance Rules
item.description: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance,...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window contains a single policy-focused development rather than an active cyber incident or vulnerability disclosure. The UK government is preparing legislation that would restrict under-16 access to major user-to-user social media platforms, with implications for platform governance, age assurance, child safety controls, and privacy debates. The UK government plans to ban children under 16 from using user-to-user social media platforms, including TikTok, Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, X, and YouTube. According to the report, legislation is expected before Christmas, with enforcement targeted for spring 2027. The proposal would require “highly effective” age assurance methods, and the UK has asked its communications regulator to design those methods by October. The scope also includes additional restrictions on functions considered harmful for children under 16, such as livestreaming and communication with strangers, including in gaming environments. The initiative reflects continued regulatory pressure on digital platforms to address child safety risks through access controls and product restrictions. Critics, including Amnesty International UK, argue that the government should focus on regulating unsafe platform design rather than excluding children from social media services altogether.
